metadata.title:
REASONS FOR THAT


CROUCH AT UNIONS

pages.raw_text:
The motive which prompts the
SpokesmanReview to attack every
move on the part Of organized labor
to secure better terms from
employers is not hard to under
stand, said local union official to-
day. THAT establishment is a
practical example Of the workings
of the open and "closed shop
The Review and Chronicle are
turned out by both union and non
union labor The union labor re-
ceives fairly remunerative wages.
some of the men being paid as high
as $500 per day. while the non
union labor working under open
shop" conditions receives as little
as $1,00 per day. When this situa-
ton is fully understood 1t is very
easy to understand why the official
organ of capital in this city is OPP
posed to the telegraphers, carmen.
miners, electrical workers or any
union which is engaged in fight
with combined CAPITAL for better
wages, hours and conditions, and
lHSlol Oll Cllloi SlHS IdW . WAlSl W III
protect them in earning a decent
livelihood for themselves and their
families.


when the Spokesman-Review
talks about illegal strikes it recalls
to the minds of some of the local
union men an occasion when the
editor of that paper then member
of the Typographical union, held
his union was a,ting in an illegal
manner, and the union made good
by taking his membership away
from him, and has since refused to
deal with him in any capacity even
when he was acting in an official
manner for the owner of his paper.
When all these facts are known the
animus of the Review can be read
lily understood


"The fact that the editor of the
Review has been held in contempt
by the members of local unions
prompts him to brand one-half of
the voters of this city criminals"
and law breakers.
